GURRENT AFFAIRS.

: Mr. Edward Martyn’s case against the Kildare Street:
Club was a great disappointment to newspaper readers.
We expected revelations and “ thrills and we only got:
legal technicalities and quibbles. The leader presump-
tive of the Irish race at home and abroad did not appear
in the box, and even Mr. Tim Healy did not enliven
the proceedings with a speech. Mr. Serjeant O'Conror,
s ‘‘tame’’ Papist, and Mr. Denis Henry, a sorb of
Orange Papist, had practically all the quibbling to
themselves on Mr. Martyn’s side.

: erson named Bailey, who, it appears, is secretary

'of%his club, deposed that Mr. Martyn “resided in the
~ club premises perhaps more frequent than almost anyoxlle
else.’”” The grammar is not ours. This evidence may help
to console those of us who have not Juxuriant castles on

the solid ground. We don’t know if Bailey waits ab
table and takes ‘‘tips,”” but the following reply to
Counsel, in cross-examination, suggests the regulation
“waitaw.”” ‘“ It was my one lapse, Serjeant, from the
impartial position. I am very sorry for it, and I think
you have very severely punished me for it.””  Poor
Bailey! The music hall rhymsters ought to write a
song about him now.

Mr. Ronan, in view of the fact that Mr. Martyn did
not go into the witness-box, referred to' him as a
““fanatic in a funk,’”’” and Mr. Matheson, in his speech,
declared : ‘“ He (Mr. Matheson) had no connection with
the army, but if the members of the club who were
made at all of the stuff he was made of—that he, a
common civilian, a lawyer, was made of—he ventured
to say that if he was an officer in the army the first
time he met- Mr. Martyn in the club after he had made
that insulting speech, all the injunctions of that Court
would not have kept him (Mr. Matheson) from laying
violent hands upon him.”” Evidently Mr. Matheson is
a fanatic with his dander up!

The defeated Unionist candidate, Mr. Denis Henry,
exculpated Mr. Martyn from the charge of being in a
funk, in that he did not go into the witness-box. It
was his Counsel, Mr. Henry said, who were alone re-
sponsible for his non-appearance in the witness-box.

Mr. Edward Martyn, even though he declared that
Trishmen who entered the British army and navy de-
served to be flogged, has been reinstated in the club of
England’s Faithful Garrison, in XKildare Street, by
force of British Law! The members of the club put
him out; he appealed to British Law, and British Law
upheld the right of Mr. Martyn to enjoy the privileges
of the club. It all seems very topsy-turvey to the plain
man, when he remembers that Mr. Edward Martyn is
President of the so-called ‘“ National Council,”” and is
in rebellion against the Empees as well as against the
Lmpire.

We should have thought that no Nationalist, least of
all a fanatic of that ilk, should belong to this club that,
in the social order, stands for what Dublin Castle stands
for in the political, and Trinity College in the educa-
tional, order. We would not go so far as to say that a
Nationalist who joined or remained a member of the
Kildare Street Club ought to be whipped. But we
cannot understand why Mr. Martyn did not signalise
his conversion to Nationalist views by resigning his,
membership of this stronghold of Irish landlordism and
brewers’ clerks. Candidly we must say that the expul-
sion of Mr. Martyn strikes us as very natural, however
illegal it may be. IHe said that several members of the
Kildare Street Club ought to be flogged ; the members,
including, we presume, some of the commissioned
Tommy Atkins that deserved flogging according to Mr.
Martyn, did not retort by flogging him, they only ex-
pelled him with beans in a ballot box. Mr. Martyn
decided that his peaceful expulsion, without the loss of
a drop of his blood and without a scratch on his nose,
was technically incorrect, according to British Law as
established in Ireland. He put the British Law in
motion with a view to asserting his technical right to
membership, and British Liaw has vindicated his rights
as against the commissioned Tommies who ought to be
flogged, “the landlords, and the brewers’ clerks.

The President of the ‘ National Council ’ is now by
virtue of British Law, the aid of which as a British sub-
ject he evoked, reinstated as an evicted tenant from the

However, he intends to

attract attention by keeping out. 
A much easier way 
of keeping out of Parliament and working at home, a 
way that is followed by millions of people even in this 
country, is to keep out without creating a rumpus by 
endeavouring to secure the right to go in. 
But that 
way is perhaps· too low-down and common for aspiring

We are rather amused spectators of such contests as 
that now going on in North Leitrim. 
Now that· Mr. 
Edward Martyn has successfully asserted, in a Brit ish

Street Club, perhaps he will take to the hustings in 
North Leitrim; and, as tillage cares probably do not 
worry Mr. John Sweetman over much just now, perhaps 
he would also lend a hand in Mr. Dolan's campaign. 
Mr. Martyn's Party has no future before them. What 
the country wants in the parliamentary way is not that 
the "floor of the House" should be deserted by the Irish_ 
Party, but rather that the fighting power and effective-

is at present constituted, or -in its semi-official organ,

We do not think that the citizens of Cork would be
over pleased if we congratulated them on one of their
newest magistrates. We see that Mr. Edward Cahill,
P.L.G., Riversdale, Sunday’s Well, has been appointed
to the Commission of the Peace for the city of Cork.
This new J.P. was a familiar figure at recent general
meetings of the G. S. & W. Railway. At one of them
he declared that  He did not think that gentlemen like
Mr. Sexton should continue this factious agitation.”
This champion of Sir Wm. Goulding, who has now been
rewarded with a J.P.-ship—it is a wonder they did not
make a knight of him as they were about it, and so
place him on a par with Sir D. Hegarty, of Cork—paid
2s. in the £ to his creditors some years ago. In our
issue of February 24th, 1906, we wrote: ‘‘ Possibly he
considers that by backing up Brother Goulding that
that ‘gentleman’ may be induced to speak to Mr.
‘Pursey Burnard,” who in turn might speak to his cousin,
Lord Bandon, who again might cause Mr. Cahill to be
made what in Cork is contemptuously called a ‘ Coal
Quay J.P.””’ And sure enough this man Cahill is a
J.P. now. This is a pretty specimen of Castle Govern-
ment with a ‘“ Liberal >’ Party in power !

We have said many sharp things in our time against
unreasoning and unfounded optimism; but if there is
one thing worse than unfounded optimism it is pes-
simism in a funk. And the latter phrase we fear de-
scribes only too accurately the attitude of a great many
people at the present juncture who ought to know better.
It is very easy for weak men to throw up their hands
and say ‘‘ the country is in a hopeless condition ’’; so
far as men of such calibre go it may be true that the

THE LEADER.

Juvy 13, 1907.

is hopeless in that they are part of it, but these
S\?e‘:xlll(bzen arepcontagious—they are a virulent contagious
disease—and their hopelessness spreads rapidly. These
human microbes lower the con§t1|3uhon of the body
national. Another class of pessimist prevalent at the
time is represented in the man who wants a complete
change of policy diet—if we may use tbsjt term—merely
because he has not got fat on the old dle!;, or as fat as
he would like to be. Well, we are not satisfied with the
conduct of our various programmes, We are not
emotional optimists, but let us record our faith that, so
far as Ireland is concerned, there is a kick in the old dog

yet.

We were glad to read a gleam 'of optimism and hope
in the remarks which Mr. T .P. Gill, of the Department,
addressed to the great North Tipperary Fgls, at Nenagh
recently. He said, according to a report in the Nenagh
Guardian, *“ That anybody who knew deeply enough of-
what is going on amongst the Irish people, and who has
courage enough to look far ahead, w:}ll see t]:lat in spite
of black spots here and there, there is still light on the
horizon. The young men and women that he saw before
him were not degenerates. Their physique was magnifi-
cent and their moral and mental stature were being im-
proved Ly the influence of the great movement in which
they were participating. The Gaelic movement was bur-
rowing into the souls of the people by every avenue the
highest influences that could be brought to bear on the
hearts of men. They were learning reverence for the
past, hope for the future, and industry and resolute
work for the present. Since the days of Father Mathew
there had not been such a wave of temperance passing
over the country, and that was largely due to the in-
fluence of the Irish-Ireland movement. The influence
of the movement upon agriculture and industries was,
perhaps, the most fruitful of all. They were aware, as
agricultural people, that they had been affected by com-
petition with Denmark. Fifty years ago Denmark was
in a far worse position than Ireland is to-day, and it
was now at the head of the market in all sorts of pro-
duce—butter and bacon especially. What enabled Den-
mark to obtain that position? They would be told co-
operation and other (hings. No doubt these things
played their part, but the moving cause that lay behind
them all was a movemert exactly like the Gaelic move-
ment in Ireland. If they followed in that path they
would not only reach prosperity, as the Danish people
had donc, hut every element of national nrogress that is
necessary for a ; ecple would be at their ccazmand.””

We wanl a few mo: > notes of that kind by responsible
men, for a shivering pessimistic nation cannot accom-
plish anything to speak of. Whatever happens, or has
happened, we must keep in the fight to win. It is not
because we have not a blind confidence in the Irish
Party or an unqualified admiration for our manufac-

We' see that Mr. John Redmond has had something
to say to Mr. Edward Martyn’s funny party. We
thoroughly agree with his twitting of Mr. Dolan for not
resigning at once. = Mr. Redmond said that Mr.
Dolan had promised to retire and contest the seat, and
it is his duty to resign without delay. Ie has chal-

that verdict is given the better.”’” That is fair enough,
and whatever sympathy Mr. Dolan may inspire on the
ground of his extreme youth and inexperience he will
not be likely to retain much of it if he dallies any longer
in his application for the Chiltern Hundreds. We

* would suggest that Mr. Edward Martyn contest the

ing financial drain, and surely a country worthy of every

We certainly would like to see a united movement on
the overtaxation robbery that goes on year by year.
But, if we may say so, we think there is a spice of the
element of panic in Mr. Donohue’s letter, and Mr.
Donohue of Enniscorthy is a public man for whom we
have great respect. We do mnot believe that if Mr.
Redmond, or anyone else, took up this robbery question
vigorously, he would become the leader of a ‘“powerful,
united and irresistible nation.”” He would merely, at
the best, be the leader of the financial relations ques-
tion, for there are more things in a nation than finance,
important;, though finance is. We do not disagree with
Mr. Donohue’s phrase ‘‘demoralised Parliamentary
Party,” but we think it an exaggeration to call it
powerless. We also think that it is an exaggeration
to describe the overtaxation question as ‘‘ the key of
the whole situation ’’; indeed, we think it is not only
an exaggeration, but an incorrect diagnosis.

We are anxious that the overtaxation spoliation
of this country should be shoved to the front, but there
is no use rushing excitedly at it. As far as we can see,
the movement, at its best, as we think we said recently,
must necessarily have more breadth than depth and in-
tensity, as it must be composed of all creeds and all
classes, and compromise amongst various sections of
Irishmen must be one of its chief notes.

By the way, talking of England’s robbery of Ire-
land, we see that some light-fingered person or persons
unknown have gone off with some valuable baubles that
were stored in Dublin Castle. We have only readthe
headings in the newspapers and have looked at pictures.
of the lost treasures that were reproduced in one of the
evening papers. We see by one of the headings in the
‘“ Freeman ’ that there is ‘ consternation in official
circles.”” The officials have cause, we admit, to be in
a state of consternation at the loss. But what a sense
of proportion we have in this country! Here we have
the press, morning and evening, in an excited state
about a few comparatively twopenny-ha’penny trinkets,
and all the while the British thief is taking some three
millions of pounds sterling out of the pockets of Ireland
every year, and there is no excitement about it! We
see that a “ Freeman '’ man was officially informed ““ at
an advanced hour this morning (Tuesday) that no
arrest has been made.””  What about arresting the
thief who takes some three millions of our money every
year? Perhaps ‘‘ Pink,” *“ Green’’ and Buff ’> would
think well of making up a few pictures about that little
theft !

An epidemic of drink! Mr. Starkie, R.M., sitting
at the Cork City Police Court, recently remarked ‘‘ that
there appeared to be an epidemic of drink in Cork at
present, and, of course, it was quite-obvious that the
cause of it was that some publicans supplied drink to
persons who had had sufficient, or more than sufficient
already. The police should use every possible effort to
discover the persons who infringed the law by supply-
ing drink to drunken people. It was a practice that
obviously largely ‘prevailed in Cork, and they saw the
evidences of it in that Court.”” One of the fourteen
cases of ‘“drunks’’ before the Court was that of a
young married woman, who had a child in her arms,
and the constable deposed that he was afraid she would
fall and that the child might be hurt. It was a charity
to arrest that woman anyway. Of all the heart-rending
sights that this world discloses it would be hard to beat
the sight of a drunken woman with an innocent help-
Jess child in her arms; the sight is enough to put a
strong man out of heart for a ‘whole day. One of the
magistrates, Mr. James Long, said:—'‘ In my Oplnion
it was the person who supplied her with the last drink
that should be here.””. Heavens, what a trade the lower

JULY 13, 1907. 
THE HURLING FINAL AT DUNGARYAN. 
· J AM somewhat late with this notice---;-the match was 
played 011 June 30th. · As, however, it was the 
1905 Championship that was being decided, it is only in 
. the nature of things to be in ai:rears with comment 011 
- the event. 
Seriously, it seems a pity that the G.A.A. 
should be so unable to keep up to time with their pro-
. gram,me. 
If Ossian were a · modern he wouldn't find 
himself so much out of it as he did, in matters athletic 
ou ~is return from Clp 11.<\ 11-05. 
~ike the question of education, the question of our 
railways meets you at every point in Ireland. It suited 
me to g~t to Dungarvan on the Friday before the match. 
On lookmg up the train time-table I made some discon-
certing discov1:1ries. 
The obvious way to get to Dun-
garvan from Dublin would be, of course, via. ,\Taterford. 
Especially as the G. S.-& Vl. line now runs without a 
break all t-he way. 
·well, if you can manage to leave 
Dublin at 9.15 a.m., you can travel Chat way. You 
must, however, cross the bridge at Waterford on foot or 
by car, and book again at the south station for the re-
mainder of the journey.' But, if you do not want to 
leave Dublin so early, you have only the 3.55 p.m. train, 
which lands you in Waterford (north)-if you are lucky 
-at 7.4 p.m., giving you six minutes in which to get 
across the bridge and to t.lie south station-a twenty 
. minutes' job-for the 7.10 p.lll. train up. In other 
words, you are not supposed to trM7el ·via \\T aterforcl to 
Dungarvan, although, as I said, the line is now con-
tinuous all the way. 
·what you are supposed to do is, 
• go around by Mallow. 
By this route the connection is 
all right either by the 9.15 a.m. or the 3 p .m. from 
Dublin, and you can depend on getting to your journey's 
end. It is for this route, too, that through tickets are 
issued. 
And thereby, perhaps, hangs the tale. 
For 
·observe. The return fares via Waterford would come to 
• 19s. 3d., whereas the scheduled fare-i.e., around by 
• Mallow-is 26s. 7d. ! To make the "tale" more obvious 
still, the fare (return) from Dublin to Waterfora is 
15s. 3d. ; to Kilmeaden1 five miles outside Waterford, it 
is 29s. 9d. ! 
. 
. 
As I am on the subj~ct, one more remark just to 
throw a little more light on our railways. 
From Belfast 
you can book to Kilkenny, or to Fermoy, but not, as far 
as I know, to any town south-east of a line between the 
two. 
Not even to Waterford! What the reason is• I 
can't make out. 
Except it be that the regulations of 
the days before the amalgamation of the southern rail-
ways are still in force, and that it is· considered too 
much trouble to revise them. 
But to come to the hurling, not since Parnell's time, 
I suppose, was there seen such a crowd in Dungarvan 
as -came for this.match. 
A young priest just home from 
America, and an enthusiastic Gael, said that he never 
expected to see such a gathering in Ireland anywhere. 
The sight certainly left yo-µ little excuse for pessimism

A MEMORY OF MERRIOV.

HE dusty road was yellow with sunlight, and the
grey stone wall of the convent semed to blink
drowsily in the summer warmth. There was a rich
peace about the Sabbath afternoon, a sense of gracious
well-being and quiet happiness, such as one is dimly
conscious of at the time and remembers with sharp
longing afterwards. I heard the sound of the portress’
shoes on the gravelled path inside the gate as she
hurried to answer our summons. As the door swung
open I turned for a moment to look beyond the roadway
to where the sca lay shimmering in the sunshine. The
yellow road came first, then the silver ribbons into
which the sunlight turned the metals of the railway,
then a smooth stretch of silver-grey sand, and, last of
all, the wrinkled surface of the sunlit sea. Far across
the Bay, Ben-Edar stood out boldly, and a sky of trans-
lucent turquoise smiled over all. There was no sound;
the sea was not near enough for one to hear the drowsy
ripple of the shoreward waves, and the leaves of the
trees that overhung the convent wall were motionless. A .
single sea-gull poised and circled in the middle-distance.
It was a day and a scene to make one thank God for the
blessing of sight, and a sense of that smote me sharply
as the portress welcomed me in. For this was a Blind
Asylum ; the good nuns ministered to the unhappy ones
of their own sex who ‘‘ have eyes to see and see not.’”
At least, I thought them unhappy in the pity of that
moment, and the thought was with me when I walked
towards the Asylum through a garden all gorgeous with
rich bloom. I thought of the sea and the sky, of the
cool green shadow and sheen of the trees, of the wealth
of lovely colour in the flowers all about me, and I ached
-with pity for those whose lives were lived here amidst
all this beauty, and who yet walked in lifelong darkness. .
But when I said something of this to the nun, who came
forward to receive me, she shook her head in reproof.
TFor a moment, indeed, her face clouded when I swung
round to point out the delicate loveliness of a garden
whose beauty was virginal, and said: ‘“ What sadness,
to live amidst all this and never to see it!’’ Then she
said quickly: ‘‘ Wait till you see them before you talk
of sadness. Most of them were always blind, and do
not miss what they have never known. And the others
—well, God is good, and He sends them compensations.’”
In truth, T have rarely seen a happier band of women
than those same blind inmates of Merrion. They were
of all ages, from those who could have mothered me
down to those who were young enough to be my
daughters—and I was but young myself. I seem to
remember some who could not have been more than four
years of age, little mites, whose quivering finger tips
ran quickly over my face when I lifted them. And
side by side with them were old grey-haired women with
whom' T joked and chatted once my nervousness hai
worn off.  For I was nervous and unstrung at fir

MANAGEMENT. 
T
O supplement the article on th!') above subject, 
which appeared in your issue of the 6th: instant, 
is my sole object in writing. 
I do not propose to dis-
cuss the general question, but merely to enlarge some-
what on a few minor points. 
I may say, however; that 
I regard it as a matter for sincere satisfaction all rounc1, 
that we seem at length in a fair way to have the simple 
• truth set before us in reference to one of the most im-
portant, as it is one of the. most complicated and 
troublesome, of contempoi·ary Irish questions. 
Most Qf the confusion surrounding the discussion of 
the managerial question is due to an oversight. ' It has 
not been sufficiently borne in mind that the clerical 
managers )lave at present a two-fold office. 
In virtue of 
t,heir pastorship they are, in their various parishes, the 
official guardians of faith and morals. To this guardian-
ship there is no limita.tion except the supervision of 
their superiors-the Bishop in the first instance, and 
ultimately the Holy See. It extends everywhere-to 
the homes of the people, to establishments of every kind 
in the parish, to schools under the .control of religious 
and to purely private schools, as well as to those subject 
to the N'ational Hoard. 
Not alone has the pastor ·a 1,ight, but it is his duty, 
to safeguard faith and morality everywhere within the 
limits of his- jurisdiction, and ,to see to it that all his 
flock, the young more particularly, are fittingly in-
structed in the truths of religion. 
If he were not 
ma.nager of a school in the technical sense, this right 
would b~ just as real in reference to such a school as it 
is at present. It is as real in the case of every other 
institution in the parish-school, orphanage, academy, 
or whatsoever else it may be-as it is in the case of the 
National School of which he is called the manager. 
The right in question is inherent in his office. It is 
God-given. He can no more forfeit it than he can be 
lawfully deprived of it. It is one over whi'ch the State 
has no control. 
Any attack upon it by .the civil 
authority would be a distinct and flagrant wrong, just 
as would be a similar attack emanating from any other 
quarter. 
And as this right is unequivocal and in-
destructible, it follows as a necessary. corollary tliat 
whatever measure of control may be necessary · for the 
effective exercise of the aforesaid right, in any parti-
cular set of circumstances, also belongs to him as a 
matter of right. 
This, of course, is so only incidentally. 
It varies in measure with varying circumstances. 
But 
under all circumstances the main right, and such in-_

ental rights as are essential to its proper exercise.
uld be fully recognised, and can neither be withhelti
or curtailed save unjustly. Beyond this, however, the
right of the manager in his capacity of pastor does not
extend. Any other powers he exercises in reference to

~ schools are not inherent in his office: they do not belong:

to it, nor spring from it, in any way.

The manager of a National School does, however
exercise other powers. e appoints the teachers, an({
performs all the: other local administrative dutios.
“Whence does he derive the powers necessary for all
this? From his appointment as manager. And who
appoints him manager? The civil authority : in®other
words, the National Board, which ultimately means
Dublin Castle and the British Government. That, of
course, means that he is in one of his capacities a sort of
local authority, though not by election, representing the
State and the tax-payers. To say, therefore, that to

. take from him powers vested in him by the British

Government would per se prejudice religious " instruc-
tion, or faith and morals, or that to advocate the trans-
fer of those powers to somebody else is per se anti-
Catholic, or anti-clerical, is mere drivel. Indeed, if the
present managers retain this power, it can only be as
the representatives of the State and the tax-payers, not
as pastors or as private individuals. If their rights,
whether inherent or merely incidental, in their pastoral
capacity be fully safeguarded, no valid objection, as far
as religion is concerned, can be urged against a transfer
_to others of the powers which the managers at present,
derive from the State. A transfer might be reasonably
opposed on practical or on educational grounds, but
certainly not on religious grounds.

A few illustrations will enable us to see more clearly
the practical bearing of what has been said.

When the writer was of school age, there were six or
seven private schools in his native parish and the parish
adjoining it; in his father’s day, before the National
Board or the National system had any existence, there
were twice as many. The Parish Priest of either parish
was not the manager of any of those schools. Religious
instruction, or the guardianship of faith and morals,
evidently did not require that he should be. The
ordinary powers of the pastoral office sufficed for all
purposes. The Parish Priest did not appoint the
teacher, who simply opened the school. Religion was
taught as a matter of course; and, if necessary, the
Parish Priest could have enforced its teaching. But it
never occurred to anybody that it should not be taught,
any more than it did that reading, writing, or arith-
metic should not be taught. So it is at present in the
Christian Schools of which the Parish Priests are not
‘managers. So also is it in such private schools as still
survive ; and the writer can testify that they are not
wholly extinct. ;

“ Any necessity there is, therefore, for any powers other
than the ordinary powers of the pastoral office in re-

g -ference to primary schools, arises solely from the stupid

insistence in this country on branding primary educa-

“tion in®the so-called National Schools as undenomina-

tional. The retention of the brand is a mere imposition,

. designed no doubt to satisfy the Nonconformist con-

science. Let there be an end of the silly and stupid

pretence, and then—well, the situation will be simplified.

JEWEL-DRIYING IN THE EAST.

HAT an appalling catastrophe! At the ‘yery
moment when the King of Great Britain and
Dublin Castle and the British Dominions beyond the
Seas, Bmperor of India (dissenting), is about to visit his *
Irish people, the tabernacle of his rule in Ireland falls
a prey to the enterprising burglar. ~ What Emmet
attempted and failed to accomplish is achieved by Bill
Sykes. Dublin Castle, the impregnable stronghold of
British Rule in Ireland, is sacked and confiscated by
the mere burglar of commerce while hundreds of armed
police dragoon the West to save sheep and bullocks from
the patriotic peasant.

Our evening contemporaries have waxed eloquent
over memories of Colonel Blood. The true significance
of the case has, apparently, escaped their attention.
Mr. Dooley, in one of his brilliant discourses, referred
to the late Queen Victoria as Queen of England and
that part of Ireland North of Sligo. Far better would
his description have been if he called the English
Sovereign monarch of that part of Dublin bounded on
one side by the Liffey and on the other by the slums.
In that sacred area British sway has ever been freely
acknowledged.  The Pale of modern times, here, at
least, no malcontent voice was raised. Loyal Ulster
in its moments of anger might threaten to kick the
crown into the Boyne. The South and West have ever
been disloyal, but Dublin Castle, through thick and
thin, amid Conservative caution and Liberal laxity,
has ket before its eyes the message of Trafalgar—
‘“ England this day must do its duty ’—and unheeding
pressure from within or without has impressed its sway
upon a subject people.

Lo! what a fall: Bryce banished! Birrell boule-
versed!! Aberdeen atrophied!!! Anthony absquatu-
lated ! Yet a common burglar from the shelf the
precious diamond steals and puts it in his pocket.
““Quis Separabit.”” Aye, Quis? And echo answers,
Quis? But if these things be done in The Castle, what
may not be done elsewhere. Another Latin phrase
occurs to us—‘‘ Quis Custodiet Custodes.”” If in the
citadel itself jewels are not safe, what security have we
for life or property elsewhere throughout the land.
Years ago it was said of an Irish Earl: ¢ All Ireland
cannot rule this man, therefore, let him rule all Ire-
land.”” Later, Forster said it of Parnell. On such
analogy, the thief who stole the diamonds from Dublin
Castle is best qualified to govern the country. The
home of Law and Order in Ireland cannot resist the
housebreaker, therefore, let the housebreaker sway the
land.

Putting aside, however, the lighter side of the matter,
we find ourselves confronted with a very serious ques-
tion. In fact, more than one question presents itself.
In the first place, the King is visiting our shores. Now
the King is an intelligent man, as far as his race will
permit, and he can hardly view without dismay that
that portion of his Irish dominion in which his rule is
absolute, and where his deputy has his home, is not more
secure from the delicate attention of the itinerant
wealth-collector than the dwelling of the humblest in
the land. Not alone that, successive judges have pro-
nounced the country crimeless, despite the energies of
twelve hundred policemen. The West’s awake, and
more than Mary call the cattle home, yet the crime of
first magnitude is perpetrated inside the walls of Dublin
Castle. Who's to blame? If this had happened in
Mayo, clearly the United Irish League were parties to
the plot. But in Dublin Sinn Fein men cannot be
implicated, for the deed is too positive. Lord Castle-
town is a member of the Gaelic League, and that body, -
